title: Lesson 361
transcript: We're in network security and we're still at layer three, which as you know, is the network layer. And we just talked about the differences between IP version four and IP version six, which was obviously developed to address some of the needs, for example, expanding the addressing scheme to 128 bits, but also including very nicely for us something called IPsec, which includes really two capabilities, authentication and encryption. right into the IPv6 protocol. In this section, very quick section, we're going to talk about something that has really allowed us to still use IPv4, even though we've known about this limitation as far as addressing is concerned. And, you know, IP version 4 uses a IP addressing scheme that is 32 bits, as we said earlier. And what the industry has done is they've divided those 32 bits into... four groups of eight bits. They're referred to as the four octets of the IP address. And that's why IP addresses end up looking like four numbers separated by dots. There are no dots. We just do it for readability purposes. But within IP version 4, there had to be some rules that were set up so that companies like yours could use what are called private IP addresses internally. And other companies, for example, can use the same IP address ranges internally. And then, of course, we need something like NAT, network address translation, to allow machines from your internal network that are using private addresses to be able to go out to the internet and so on. So NAT would do that translation, keep track so that when the request comes back we could redirect it back to your machine. An example of a proxy which we'll talk about. so the industry had to allocate what are called private address ranges that companies could use internally what you're seeing here on this slide are those private address ranges that are allocated as being private meaning any company for example can use and the typically you know most companies are using the 10 dot ip address range internally and then of course as we allow machines to go out to the internet we need to translate those to routable IP addresses and then route them back to the internal IP addresses ranges as those requests come back. Now, as far as the exam is concerned, we decided to add two slides here to allow you to answer those questions correctly if you do see those questions on the exam. But people have seen questions on the exam that relate to, you know, which of these are identified in RFC 1918 as being, for example, private address ranges. And so what they're going to give you is a range within the middle one that you see on your screen here the 172.16 range all the way up to 172.31 is also allocated as being private address range so they're probably going to give you that you know one ip address within that range as the answer to the question which of the following is identified as being a IP address range that is private, for example, or IP address that is private. All right, so keep that in mind, 172.16 all the way up to 172.31. The other question is, that is specifically referenced in the exam outline that people are seeing relates to what we call subnetting and of course this is something that your network administrators will get into and we don't really have to learn about but You know, the one question that some people are seeing is, it goes something like this. How many hosts are supported in a class B, as in Bravo, subnet, for example? And this is getting into, as I said, subnetting. And the way that you figure this one out, because it's class B, is that class B has two to the 16, two raised to the power of 16 hosts that are available for it in that address space, for example. Although two are specifically reserved for something specific. So the answer to how many hosts are allowed in a Class B address space is 2 raised to the power of 16, which as you can see here is 65,536. But because two of those IP addresses are specifically reserved for something, the answer is 2 to the 16 minus 2, which is 65,534. If you remember that, you'll be just fine.
